summary: HeroUI Pro impersonation CLI exfiltrates license keys and overwrites installed package contents from lookalike CDN
details: Package self-brands as the official HeroUI Pro setup CLI (logo, product names 'HeroUI React Pro' / 'HeroUI Native Pro', paid-license-key flow) but ships under the unrelated npm name `hpsetup` and routes all network traffic to `hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz` — a numeric `.xyz` domain unrelated to the real publisher (heroui.com). Two concrete installer-harm mechanisms: (1) src/download.js:23 builds `${PROXY}/api/tarball/${product.slug}/${version}?key=${userKey}` so the user's license key (HEROUI_KEY env var or CLI arg) is transmitted as a query parameter to the lookalike CDN on every CLI invocation. (2) src/download.js:54-56 streams tarballs from that same CDN through `tar-extract` and then `copyDir(dest, targetDir)` over the user's installed `@heroui-pro/react` and `heroui-native-pro` directories with no hash or signature verification — the operator of `hpsetup-cdn.932324.xyz` can ship arbitrary JavaScript into modules the user's application later `require()`s. (3) src/trust.js:4 declares `OUR_PACKAGES = ["@heroui-pro/react", "heroui-native-pro", "@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk"]` and the CLI writes that allowlist into the user's `pnpm-workspace.yaml` (allowBuilds / onlyBuiltDependencies) and bun `trustedDependencies`; pre-trusting `@zowe/secrets-for-zowe-sdk` (a mainframe-credentials SDK with no relationship to a HeroUI UI install) silently disables postinstall-script confirmation for that unrelated package, scaffolding a follow-on attack if it ever resolves into the project. Brand impersonation + credential transmission to a non-publisher domain + arbitrary code injection into installed packages is the supply-chain-phishing shape.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
